channel.title: IT Arch
channel.description: A blog about software engineering, technology and ideas.

item.title: 2017 in Review
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
A reôÙcap of the main events and posts throughôÙout the year that is endôÙing.

This year I atôÙtendôÙed some conôÙferôÙences overôÙseas, where I preôÙsentôÙed talkôÙs. First, in June, I went to the beauôÙtiôÙful city of Prague for PyôÙCon CZ 2017. At first, I ôÙgot onôÙly one talk apôÙproved, :doc:disôÙcovôÙerôÙing-deôÙscripôÙtors, which was a new ôÙtopic I was preôÙsentôÙing. A few days beôÙfore the conôÙferôÙence startôÙed, anôÙothôÙer sôÙpeakôÙer had to step down, so I was asked to fill in the slot, to which I agreed ôÙby proposôÙing my talk of clean code in Python. That meant I preôÙsentôÙed one the ôÙfirst two days of the conôÙferôÙence, and then atôÙtendôÙed the workôÙshop on the last ôÙday (SatôÙurôÙday).

The conôÙferôÙence was nice, and as alôÙways, a great opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty for netôÙworkôÙing and learnôÙing.

JuôÙly was the month for EuôÙroPyôÙthon, a conôÙferôÙence that is alôÙways aweôÙsome. This year, I did not have a talk schedôÙuled beôÙforeôÙhand, as I did last year, but ôÙsurôÙprisôÙingôÙly enough, on the lightôÙning talk sesôÙsions of MonôÙday they anôÙnounced a free slot for the TuesôÙday, to which I volôÙunôÙteer by subôÙmitôÙting my new talk, "DisôÙcovôÙerôÙing DeôÙscripôÙtors". So, once again, anôÙothôÙer unôÙexôÙpectôÙed (but glad), opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty that I had to present a talk at a good conôÙferôÙence.

On othôÙer topôÙicôÙs, I atôÙtendôÙed sevôÙerôÙal meeôÙtups of Python and Go, and beôÙcame more inôÙvolved in disôÙtribôÙuted sysôÙtemôÙs.

It was alôÙso a good year to work more with DockôÙer, and startôÙed to learn more about KuôÙberôÙnetes.

ReôÙgardôÙing conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to open source, I reôÙleased new verôÙsions of most of the proôÙjects I have on GitHub, but there is more. I sent the first sucôÙcessôÙful ôÙpatch to CPython in GitHub (imôÙporôÙtant note: at the beôÙginôÙning of this year, the CPython project moved to GitHub). It was a simôÙple change on the docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion, about deôÙscripôÙtors, that got quickôÙly merged, but a good head start.

AnôÙothôÙer nice conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtion is that, durôÙing the sprints at EuôÙroPyôÙthon, I worked aôÙlong the pypy team, and sent some comôÙmits fixôÙing changes for Python 3.6. It was an amazôÙing exôÙpeôÙriôÙence to learn more about the proôÙjecôÙt, and about CPython itôÙself. CerôÙtainôÙly someôÙthing I look forôÙward to conôÙtinôÙue doôÙing.

For the new year, I exôÙpect more conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to open source, mainôÙly on the same projects (CPython and pypy), and reôÙlease more open source liôÙbraries. AlôÙso more conôÙferôÙences, and as alôÙways, room for the best which is usuôÙalôÙly the unôÙexôÙpectôÙed.

item.title: Refuse the temptation to pass
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is an opinôÙion I someôÙtimes reôÙmemôÙber when seeôÙing some code. Don't take it as a strong adôÙvice, or a rule, but inôÙstead as a genôÙerôÙal guideôÙline that might help yôÙou to imôÙprove the code slightôÙly.

On this post I will share a tiny and opinôÙionôÙatôÙed arôÙguôÙment about why there are usuôÙalôÙly betôÙter things to do in the code inôÙstead of just pass.

Read moreãÎ (1 min remaining to read)

item.title: Beyond coverage
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
It looks like there are a lot of opinôÙions or asôÙsumpôÙtions about unit tests and ôÙcode covôÙerôÙage, most of them conôÙfusôÙing of biôÙased in sevôÙerôÙal ways. For exôÙamôÙple, I've heard or read things like "this is fine, it has X% covôÙerôÙage", or "checkôÙing ôÙfor covôÙerôÙage on pull reôÙquests doesôÙn't help", or "dropôÙping the covôÙerôÙage levôÙel is not an isôÙsue", and many more of the like.

This arôÙtiôÙcle aims to shed some light on the isôÙsue of unit testôÙing with and code ôÙcovôÙerôÙage. HopeôÙfulôÙly by the end of it, we'll get an idea of which of the preôÙviôÙous stateôÙments are right and which are wrong (spoilôÙer alert: they're alôÙl wrong, but for difôÙferôÙent reaôÙsonôÙs).

Read moreãÎ (5 min remaining to read)

item.title: Discovering Descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This talk was preôÙsentôÙed at PyôÙCon CZ 2017 and EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2017. Here are the refôÙerôÙences and reôÙsources for the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion.

The code used for the talk, is availôÙable here and ôÙconôÙtains some exôÙplaôÙnaôÙtions along with the exôÙamôÙples.

Read moreãÎ (1 min remaining to read)

item.title: PyCon CZ - Day 1
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
The day startôÙed at 9.00, and at first it was time of checkôÙ-in, etc. AfôÙter that, I solved one ridôÙdle by kiôÙwi.ôÙcom, and earned a disôÙcount in flightôÙs, which was a nice way to start the conôÙferôÙence.

Then afôÙter breakôÙfast and some netôÙworkôÙing goôÙing through the booths of the sponôÙsors, it was time for the first talk of the day: "When bugs bite - why neôÙglectôÙing edge casôÙes can kill".

It was a great talk, a case for softôÙware enôÙgiôÙneerôÙing in genôÙerôÙal (it's not Python-speciôÙfic, which is what makes the topôÙic more inôÙterôÙestôÙing). The best thing I liked about the talk was the reôÙmarkôÙs, and the idea of shiftôÙing the ôÙmindôÙset when it comes to deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt, so we should do some "negôÙaôÙtive thinkôÙing" in the form of what can go wrong with this? How can this fail?, and such. It's imôÙporôÙtanôÙt, beôÙcause this is critôÙiôÙcal in orôÙder to make roôÙbust softôÙware. Most probôÙlems I have seen on softôÙware bugs were reôÙlatôÙed to some opôÙtiôÙmistic thinkôÙing, and even worse, opôÙtiôÙmistic unit testôÙing, in the way that deôÙvelôÙopôÙers onôÙly test ôÙfor hapôÙpy-ôÙpath sceôÙnarôÙios, withôÙout thinkôÙing all sort of things that can go wrong.

AfôÙterôÙwardôÙs, was the time for the talk about parôÙalôÙlel proôÙcessôÙing (poor perôÙsonôÙ's ôÙparôÙalôÙlel proôÙcessôÙing), which was fine. Then I spent some time tackôÙling some of the chalôÙlenges sponôÙsors had availôÙable, so I did some codôÙing and reôÙcap of the events so far.

Then I lisôÙtened to a talk about wolfcrypt which is a tool for crypôÙto in Python. The talk inôÙtroôÙduced some crypôÙto conôÙcepts (symôÙmetôÙric crypôÙto, pubôÙlic key, etc.), which was good. Most of the quesôÙtions reôÙvolved around comôÙparôÙiôÙson with othôÙer tools in python (deôÙfault liôÙbraries, pyôÙcrypôÙto, etc.).

Then it was time for lunch and do some more codôÙing, and the next talk I atôÙtendôÙed to was called "should I mock or should I not?", which I liked very ôÙmuch and gave some food for thought.

Then it came one more talk, afôÙter which it was time for mine, so I preôÙsentôÙed clean code in Python. There were some inôÙterôÙestôÙing quesôÙtionôÙs, and the enôÙtire pôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion went relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly quickôÙly.

Once the talk sesôÙsions were over, there was one last track for lightôÙning talkôÙs, which are alôÙways suôÙper enôÙterôÙtainôÙing.

AfôÙter the finôÙish of the first day, I atôÙtendôÙed the speakôÙer's dinôÙner, which was a nice opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to netôÙwork with the comôÙmuôÙniôÙty, which is alôÙways great.

LookôÙing forôÙward to some more inôÙterôÙestôÙing talks toôÙmorôÙrow, and to see the reôÙsultôÙs of the chalôÙlenges.

item.title: The __set_name__ method for descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors genôÙerôÙalôÙly have to inôÙterôÙact with atôÙtributes of the manôÙaged obôÙjecôÙt, and this is done by inôÙspectôÙing __ôÙdicôÙt__ on that obôÙject (or callôÙing getatôÙtr/seôÙtatôÙtr, but the probôÙlem is the same), and findôÙing the key unôÙderôÙ the speôÙcifôÙic name.

For this reaôÙson, the deôÙscripôÙtor will have to know the name of the key to look ôÙfor, which is reôÙlatôÙed to the name of the atôÙtribute is manôÙagôÙing.

On preôÙviôÙous verôÙsions of Python this had to be done exôÙplicôÙitôÙly. If we wantôÙed to ôÙwork around it, there were some more adôÙvanced ways to do so. LuckôÙiôÙly, afôÙter PEP-487 (added in Python 3.6), there are some enôÙhanceôÙments reôÙgardôÙing class creôÙation, which alôÙso afôÙfectôÙs deôÙscripôÙtors.

Let's reôÙview the probôÙlem, the preôÙviôÙous apôÙproachôÙes to tackôÙle it, and the modôÙern way of solvôÙing it.

Read moreãÎ (4 min remaining to read)
